title: The Human-AI Interaction Bias Codex
subtitle: A denser Wikipedia-backed map of human-AI interaction distortions, including interaction-relevant cognitive and algorithmic bias terms.

quadrants.title: We trust AI too much or too little

statements.label: We hand too much judgment over to the system

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: We hand too much judgment over to the system
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The tendency to favor automated suggestions and ignore contradictory non-automated information.
leaves.interaction_rationale: This is the canonical human-AI overtrust page: users overweight automated outputs and underweight independent checks.
cross_links: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: We hand too much judgment over to the system
leaves.one_sentence_definition: This section of the automation bias literature distinguishes ignoring automation from overusing it in the wrong conditions.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Section-backed replacement for automation misuse: it directly names the misuse branch of automation failure in a canonical Wikipedia source.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: We hand too much judgment over to the system
leaves.one_sentence_definition: This section describes mistakes made by following bad automated advice or by failing to act when the system stays silent.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Section-backed replacement for algorithm appreciation: it captures the concrete error pattern that occurs when users defer too readily to automated output.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: We hand too much judgment over to the system
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Repeated experiences of lacking control can reduce initiative and independent action.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful for overdependence on AI when repeated reliance weakens the user’s willingness to think or act independently.

statements.label: Authority and polish make AI feel more trustworthy

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Authority and polish make AI feel more trustworthy
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The tendency to give extra weight to opinions or outputs associated with authority.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Explains why expert framing, system labels, or confident AI delivery can trigger deference.
cross_links: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Authority and polish make AI feel more trustworthy
leaves.one_sentence_definition: A form of reasoning that treats an authority’s claim as evidence that the claim is true.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when users accept AI output because it sounds authoritative rather than because they evaluated the evidence.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Authority and polish make AI feel more trustworthy
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Information that is easier to process is often judged as more credible or preferable.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because polished, smooth AI answers can feel more trustworthy even when they are not more accurate.
cross_links: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Authority and polish make AI feel more trustworthy
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Source credibility refers to a communicator's qualities that affect whether a receiver accepts a message.
leaves.interaction_rationale: More specific than generic credibility: it captures how users accept or reject AI output based on the perceived trustworthiness of the source.

statements.label: Once AI feels reliable, we stop checking it

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Once AI feels reliable, we stop checking it
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Performance can decline when automation removes people from active control and monitoring.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Directly describes what happens when users supervise AI less actively after they come to trust it.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Once AI feels reliable, we stop checking it
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Sustained attention is needed to detect infrequent signals over time.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits because AI-assisted workflows often still require careful monitoring that users let lapse.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Once AI feels reliable, we stop checking it
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The perception and understanding of relevant elements in a changing environment.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here when AI tools weaken the user’s active grasp of the task by taking over too much monitoring.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Once AI feels reliable, we stop checking it
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Unexpected automated behavior that catches the user off guard when the system acts differently than anticipated.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits the trust-failure pattern where users lose track of what the automation is doing and are surprised by its behavior.

statements.label: We push back when AI feels controlling or unreliable

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: We push back when AI feels controlling or unreliable
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The tendency to distrust or reject algorithmic advice, especially after observing mistakes.
leaves.interaction_rationale: This is the clearest page for under-trust and backlash against AI assistance.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: We push back when AI feels controlling or unreliable
leaves.one_sentence_definition: A perceived threat to autonomy can trigger resistance to persuasion or control.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when people resist AI recommendations because the system feels intrusive or controlling.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: We push back when AI feels controlling or unreliable
leaves.one_sentence_definition: A judge–advisor system is a decision structure used to study how people take, revise, or discount advice from an adviser.
leaves.interaction_rationale: A real Wikipedia page for advice-taking research that fits under AI distrust when users underweight external machine advice relative to their own judgment.

statements.label: Legitimacy and control shape whether we accept AI

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Legitimacy and control shape whether we accept AI
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The technology acceptance model explains how perceived usefulness and ease of use shape whether people adopt a technology.
leaves.interaction_rationale: More specific than generic trust: it captures the user-side acceptance process that shapes whether people rely on AI systems at all.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Legitimacy and control shape whether we accept AI
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Fairness in machine learning concerns attempts to detect and correct unfair patterns in ML-driven decisions.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Included here as an interaction-relevant legitimacy concept: users calibrate trust partly through whether a system appears fair or biased.

leaves.quadrant: We trust AI too much or too little
leaves.statement: Legitimacy and control shape whether we accept AI
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Locus of control is the degree to which people believe outcomes depend on their own actions rather than external forces.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits the felt-control side of AI reliance: people respond differently when they experience the system as controlling outcomes rather than supporting their own agency.

quadrants.title: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
quadrants.failure_point: how our prior beliefs shape what we take from AI

statements.label: We interpret AI through what we already think

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: We interpret AI through what we already think
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People search for, interpret, and recall information in ways that support what they already believe.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Core page for why users turn to AI to validate prior beliefs and read its outputs selectively.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: We interpret AI through what we already think
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Information is evaluated in ways that serve desired conclusions or identities.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when users accept agreeable AI answers and rationalize away the rest.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: We interpret AI through what we already think
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Reasoning is judged by whether the conclusion seems believable rather than by whether the logic is sound.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful when plausible-sounding AI output is accepted because it fits a prior belief, not because the reasoning is strong.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: We interpret AI through what we already think
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People are quicker to detect bias in others than in themselves.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because users may see AI or other people as biased while overlooking their own selective uptake of AI output.

statements.label: We seek friendly answers and filter out the rest

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: We seek friendly answers and filter out the rest
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People tend to choose information that reinforces their existing views.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Directly maps to prompting, searching, and iterating with AI until it yields a preferred answer.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: We seek friendly answers and filter out the rest
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People more readily notice and remember information that fits existing expectations.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when users highlight the parts of an AI answer that confirm their view and ignore the rest.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: We seek friendly answers and filter out the rest
leaves.one_sentence_definition: A belief can persist even after strong contrary evidence appears.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because AI contradiction often fails to change a user’s position once they are committed to it.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: We seek friendly answers and filter out the rest
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Only the evidence that supports a preferred conclusion is selected while conflicting evidence is ignored.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when users pull validating examples from AI output while discarding counterexamples or caveats.

statements.label: Repeated exposure makes some claims feel truer

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: Repeated exposure makes some claims feel truer
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Beliefs are amplified when repeated inside a closed, like-minded information environment.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because AI-mediated feeds and conversational loops can repeatedly surface the same viewpoint.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: Repeated exposure makes some claims feel truer
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Personalized curation can isolate users inside a narrower information environment.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits personalization-driven AI systems that keep serving what already matches the user’s profile or history.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: Repeated exposure makes some claims feel truer
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Repeated claims can feel truer even when repetition adds no evidence.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful when AI restates or rephrases a claim often enough that it feels more established.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: Repeated exposure makes some claims feel truer
leaves.one_sentence_definition: A belief gains plausibility and spread through repetition and social reinforcement.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when AI systems amplify already-circulating narratives until they seem self-validating.

statements.label: AI can harden stereotypes and category shortcuts

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: AI can harden stereotypes and category shortcuts
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The stereotype content model explains stereotypes in terms of perceived warmth and competence.
leaves.interaction_rationale: More specific than a generic stereotype page: it captures the structured warmth-competence template through which AI outputs can reinforce social impressions.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: AI can harden stereotypes and category shortcuts
leaves.one_sentence_definition: An implicit stereotype is a pre-reflective attribution of group-linked qualities to others.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when AI outputs trigger or reinforce unconscious group associations rather than explicit reasoning.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: AI can harden stereotypes and category shortcuts
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Out-group homogeneity is the tendency to see out-group members as more similar to one another than in-group members.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful for understanding how people and systems flatten diverse groups into overly uniform categories.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: AI can harden stereotypes and category shortcuts
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The representativeness heuristic is judging probability or category membership by similarity to a typical case rather than by base rates.
leaves.interaction_rationale: More specific than a generic schema page: AI outputs can feel right when they match a familiar type or stereotype, even when the evidence is weak.
cross_links: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames

statements.label: Biased data patterns can confirm what people expect

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: Biased data patterns can confirm what people expect
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Algorithmic bias describes systematic and repeatable unfair tendencies in computerized sociotechnical systems.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Included here because biased AI outputs can feed back into user beliefs and make historical patterns look objective.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: Biased data patterns can confirm what people expect
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Selection bias is distortion caused by how people, groups, or data are chosen for analysis.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when an AI system appears to confirm a story because the underlying data were skewed from the start.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: Biased data patterns can confirm what people expect
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Sampling bias occurs when some members of the intended population are more likely to be included than others.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because non-representative training or evaluation data can make AI outputs reinforce a partial view of the world.

leaves.quadrant: We use AI to confirm what we already believe
leaves.statement: Biased data patterns can confirm what people expect
leaves.one_sentence_definition: A proxy variable stands in for an unobservable target and can distort conclusions when it is treated as if it were the real thing.
leaves.interaction_rationale: A better Wikipedia-backed fit for AI measurement problems: systems often optimize proxies that users then mistake for the real variable of interest.

quadrants.title: AI's suggestions steer our choices
quadrants.research_title: Judgment Steering by Anchors and Frames
quadrants.failure_point: how AI presentation shapes our choices

statements.label: Starting cues and easy signals steer judgment

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Starting cues and easy signals steer judgment
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Judgments are pulled toward an initial reference point or suggestion.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Core page for AI draft answers, first numbers, and initial recommendations shifting later judgment.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Starting cues and easy signals steer judgment
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People replace a hard judgment with an easier heuristic attribute without noticing.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when an AI interface supplies an easy cue and users substitute it for a harder evaluation.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Starting cues and easy signals steer judgment
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People judge importance or likelihood by how easily examples come to mind.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because surfaced examples and highlighted cases can steer decisions more than base rates.

statements.label: Wording and fluency change what feels right

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Wording and fluency change what feels right
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Decisions can change when equivalent options are presented in different ways.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Directly captures how AI wording and presentation alter judgments without changing the underlying information.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Wording and fluency change what feels right
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Easy-to-process information feels more familiar, trustworthy, or true.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when AI outputs persuade by being cleanly phrased and cognitively easy rather than substantively better.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Wording and fluency change what feels right
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Repeated exposure can increase familiarity and liking on its own.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because repeated interface exposure can make certain options feel safer or more appealing.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Wording and fluency change what feels right
leaves.one_sentence_definition: A vivid individual case can evoke stronger judgment than abstract statistics.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful when AI surfaces vivid anecdotes or examples that outweigh broader evidence in decision-making.

statements.label: Preselected options become the path of least resistance

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Preselected options become the path of least resistance
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People disproportionately stick with preselected or default options.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits AI interfaces that prefill, pre-rank, or preselect an answer path for the user.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Preselected options become the path of least resistance
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People prefer maintaining the current or default state of affairs.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because users often stay with the system’s initial setup rather than reconsider it.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Preselected options become the path of least resistance
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The design of how options are presented can shape later decisions.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful as the umbrella concept for AI interfaces quietly structuring judgment and action.

statements.label: How option sets and nudges change what feels best

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: How option sets and nudges change what feels best
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Adding an asymmetrically inferior option can shift preferences between the original options.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits recommendation sets where AI-added comparisons steer the user toward a target option.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: How option sets and nudges change what feels best
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Overchoice is the phenomenon where too many options make decision-making harder or worse.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits AI interfaces that present many generated or ranked options, making overload itself part of how the system steers the user.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: How option sets and nudges change what feels best
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Judgment of an option can change depending on the surrounding context or alternatives.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful when AI presentation makes an option look stronger or weaker by changing the comparison frame.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: How option sets and nudges change what feels best
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Behavior can be influenced by structuring the choice environment without removing options.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits deliberate AI interface design that steers user behavior while preserving nominal choice.

statements.label: Attention and popularity cues can outweigh better evidence

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Attention and popularity cues can outweigh better evidence
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The tendency to notice some cues more readily than competing cues.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because highlighted interface elements and repeated signals dominate user attention.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Attention and popularity cues can outweigh better evidence
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Earlier stimuli can influence later judgments or responses without conscious intent.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits prompt wording, examples, and prior AI turns that shift later decisions before reflection.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Attention and popularity cues can outweigh better evidence
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People copy the actions or choices of others to decide what to do.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful when popularity cues, consensus counts, or “others chose this” signals make AI options feel correct.

leaves.quadrant: AI's suggestions steer our choices
leaves.statement: Attention and popularity cues can outweigh better evidence
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Recent information can be weighted more heavily than earlier information.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits conversational AI contexts where the latest answer or last-ranked item crowds out better earlier evidence.

quadrants.title: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
quadrants.failure_point: how we misunderstand what the AI is

statements.label: Human-like cues make AI feel more alive than it is

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: Human-like cues make AI feel more alive than it is
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The attribution of human traits, motives, or feelings to non-human entities.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Core page for treating AI systems as if they had human traits or inner lives.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: Human-like cues make AI feel more alive than it is
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Human-like feelings, intentions, or behavioral traits are attributed specifically to AI systems.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Directly names the AI-specific version of the human-likeness attribution problem.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: Human-like cues make AI feel more alive than it is
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Biological motion is movement that humans perceive as coming from a living organism.
leaves.interaction_rationale: More specific than the linguistics page on animacy: lifelike motion cues in robots and avatars can make AI seem animate and intentional.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: Human-like cues make AI feel more alive than it is
leaves.one_sentence_definition: The uncanny valley effect describes strong responses to entities that appear almost human but not fully so.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because near-human AI cues can intensify mind-perception errors rather than simply reducing them.

statements.label: Conversation makes software feel socially present

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: Conversation makes software feel socially present
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People can attribute understanding or empathy to a conversational program beyond what it warrants.
leaves.interaction_rationale: This is the canonical page for mistaking conversational smoothness for actual understanding.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: Conversation makes software feel socially present
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People often apply human social rules and expectations to computers and software agents.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits human-AI interaction directly: users respond socially to systems that only simulate social cues.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: Conversation makes software feel socially present
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Digital interfaces can create a stronger sense that another social presence is really there.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because AI chat systems can feel socially present in ways that distort user judgment.

statements.label: We read goals, minds, and motives into AI behavior

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We read goals, minds, and motives into AI behavior
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Behavior is interpreted by ascribing beliefs, goals, and intentions to an entity.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful for explaining why people read motives into AI output that only reflects patterns and system design.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We read goals, minds, and motives into AI behavior
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Theory of mind is the capacity to understand others by ascribing mental states to them.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when ordinary mind-reading habits are extended to AI systems that do not actually possess human-like minds.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We read goals, minds, and motives into AI behavior
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Agent detection is the inclination to presume purposeful intervention by an intelligent or sentient agent.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because users may infer a deliberate agent behind ambiguous AI behavior or outputs.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We read goals, minds, and motives into AI behavior
leaves.one_sentence_definition: A person mistakes subjective judgments or interpretations for objective properties of the world.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when users project intention or understanding onto AI as if those impressions were intrinsic system properties.

statements.label: We see patterns and coherence that the system does not have

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We see patterns and coherence that the system does not have
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Meaningful patterns or agency are perceived in ambiguous stimuli where none may exist.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful when users read emotion, purpose, or message into ambiguous machine output.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We see patterns and coherence that the system does not have
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Apophenia is the tendency to perceive meaningful connections between unrelated things.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because users often infer hidden depth or intent from coincidental AI behavior.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We see patterns and coherence that the system does not have
leaves.one_sentence_definition: A mental model is an internal representation of how a system works.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when users fill in unknown details about AI with oversimplified or incorrect internal models.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We see patterns and coherence that the system does not have
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People often overestimate the accuracy of their own explanations for their judgments and mental processes.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Useful when users become too confident in their own story about how the AI reached an answer.

statements.label: We overestimate how deeply AI actually understands

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We overestimate how deeply AI actually understands
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People often believe they understand a topic more deeply than they actually do.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits the tendency to mistake a convincing AI explanation for genuine deep understanding by either the user or the system.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We overestimate how deeply AI actually understands
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Explainable AI is a field concerned with making AI reasoning or behavior more understandable to humans.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Included here as an interaction-relevant illusion risk: explanation can improve oversight, but it can also create a false sense that the system is fully understood.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We overestimate how deeply AI actually understands
leaves.one_sentence_definition: People can overestimate how much control they have over events and systems.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Fits when prompt tweaking or interface interaction makes users think they understand and control the AI more than they do.

leaves.quadrant: We mistake AI for understanding more than it does
leaves.statement: We overestimate how deeply AI actually understands
leaves.one_sentence_definition: Subjective confidence is often greater than objective accuracy.
leaves.interaction_rationale: Belongs here because users can become too certain about what an AI knows or can do.
